data.title: Wise: Focus On The Long-Term Fundamentals, Ignore Near-Term Noise
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Wise Plc (OTCPK:WPLCF) previously, as I believed the business had a clear growth runway, and it has a solid edge to continue capturing demand. My stance on WPLCF is still a buy rating. The Platform business is scaling nicely as expected; WPLCF is also widening its moat, and customer growth remains strong.

Wise Platform Has Hit a Critical Inflection Point

Six months ago, my view on the Platform segment was that it could eventually represent a sizeable portion of the business. Fast forward to today, I still believe my view is right, and the most recent data point did show that Platform now makes up a little above 5% of total cross-border volume. This may look non-significant, but I assure you it is. The focus here is the pace of growth, which implies that the segment is extremely fast at ~80% y/y growth (you can back into this math by using last year's share of total cross-border volume and the increase in total volume growth).

I believe this strong growth rate also tells us that the banks are effectively acknowledging that WPLCF cross-border infrastructure is a better solution than their in-house or competing offerings. The halo effect of winning more major partners also reinforces the network effect, in that by landing one major institution, it draws interest from others, and that makes each new partnership a catalyst for the next.

Also, like I said before, since these integrations sit on top of WPLCF's existing rails and require limited incremental sales effort once they go live, the incremental volume comes with very little incremental cost. And by that definition, if Platform moves to management’s 10% medium-term target, the mix shift should support margin expansion.

Widening the Moat

While only six months have passed since my initiation, WPLCF has managed to widen its moat. Specifically, its cross-border rails infrastructure moat. To give a better sense of things, as of the latest number, 74% of transactions settle in under 20 seconds, which is up from 63% a year ago. For context, in cross-border payments, to achieve that level of speed, one must bypass the traditional network and be connected directly into domestic payment systems. WPLCF now has eight of these direct connections, with Brazil’s Pix already live and Japan’s Zengin coming online next.

This advantage also shows up in the unit economics. If we look at the latest numbers, WPLCF has managed to cut its average cross-border take rate by 10 bps y/y to 0.52%, and yet the underlying gross profit margin still expanded to 76.2%. My view is very simple here. Get the cost down and drive in volume so much that the operating leverage (cost per transaction) outweighs the cut in take rate. And this is playing out as expected; the lower take rate has resulted in more volume (up 24% to GBP 84.9 billion in 1H26). As WPLCF keeps this up, the gap between it and sub-scale peers continues to widen, and at some point, it would almost be impossible to replicate this moat. If the volume data is not enough, the customer growth data also reinforces the same story. WPLCF added 3.5 million new active customers in 1H26, up 14% y/y, now with a total active customer count of 13.4 million.

Near-Term Noise

The market clearly doesn’t share my optimism since the share price is down a bunch since the earnings and my initiation. From what I am seeing, it seems to be the WPLCF margin compression aspect that is impacting sentiment. Despite all the growth and whatnot, underlying PBT margins declined sharply to 16.3% from 22.2% last year. This has probably spooked some investors and caused the sell-down. However, I don’t think this is a sign of weakening fundamentals. The unit economics have improved as mentioned above; the decline is due to WPLCF reinvesting ahead of demand. It makes all the sense in the world for WPLCF to invest, given the growth and scale advantage. What WPLCF specifically did was to hire more people (>1,000 people in 1H, which is already above the full-year target) to further improve the infrastructure (e.g., compliance, product, etc.).

You can already see the payoff in the revenue mix. Non-cross-border income now accounts for 41% of total underlying revenue, showing that WPLCF is successfully moving customers from remittance usage (which is occasional and not predictable) to broader international banking activity (the more use cases, the more volume WPLCF can tap into). To put some numbers in context, card revenue grew 28% y/y to £132 million, and customer balances grew 37% to >GBP25 billion. These metrics clearly point to deeper engagement and a higher lifetime value per customer, which supports the decision to invest more upfront in people and product.

I must also point out that the margin noise is purely optical. WPLCF absorbed GBP35 million of one-off expenses tied to its planned US dual listing. If we adjust for these costs, the underlying PBT margin would have been 17.8% in 1H26, which is well within the long-term target range.

Valuation

I am not too worried about the noise that the market is focusing on. The fundamentals remain strong, and the decision to invest ahead makes a lot of sense. The management team is clearly playing the long-term game rather than focusing on the quarterly fluctuations. I’d place my bet on a team like this. As such, I am not changing my model assumptions. I still model high-teen growth forward and margins to expand as revenue scales. The only thing I changed is to upgrade the NTM PE to 29x (the average over the past 3 years). I think WPLCF deserves an average given these improved fundamentals.

Risk

The risk I see is the heavy investment cycle may compress margins longer than expected. I get that WPLCF is betting heavily on future growth. But if top-line growth disappoints, my worry is the market will punish the stock further. Another risk is stablecoin adoption. While management dismisses this as an immediate threat, rapid stablecoin adoption could theoretically bypass fiat rails entirely, and that challenges WPLCF's core value proposition in the long run.

Conclusion

I stay buy-rated for WPLCF. The margin compression problem is temporary, while the fundamentals continue to get better (platform expansion, stronger unit economics, and deeper customer relationships). Management is investing ahead of demand, and the early payoff is already visible in mix and scale. I believe high-teen growth is realistic, and margins should expand as volumes grow.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ESPO: A Focused Global Play On The Gaming And ESport Market
data.description:
Introduction to The VanEck Video Gaming and eSports ETF

VanEck, a New York-based investment management firm that is noted for its niche ETFs, currently offers investors the opportunity to participate in the broad Video Gaming and eSports universe in the shape of a $400 million-sized product. The VanEck Video Gaming and eSports ETF (ESPO) has been around since October 2018, and currently has an expense ratio of 0.56%. ESPO, which currently consists of 25 global stocks, also makes distributions once a year, with the yield working to 0.33%

What Does ESPO Do?

ESPO is not an actively managed ETF; rather it aims to replicate an index called the MVIS Global Video Gaming and eSports Index [MGVGEI], which is designed to cover some of the largest and most liquid stocks in the global video gaming and eSports (a subcategory in sports that basically deals with competitive organized video gaming) industries.

To make it to this index, global stocks are required to generate at least half their revenue from the activities linked to video gaming (this could include not just game development, but also software and hardware related to gaming, streaming services) and eSports events. Besides this revenue requirement, stocks are also required to clear certain liquidity (Average 3-month daily trading volumes of $1 million, and 250 thousand shares traded per month over a 6-month duration) and market-cap screeners (minimum of $150 million). Currently, MGVGEI focuses on 25 stocks where the maximum weight of each stock is capped at 8% of the portfolio, and is carried out 4 times in a year.

]jh≠./x;,Main Characteristics of ESPO’s Portfolio

The first thing to be said is that this is a very narrow portfolio of only 25 stocks, and an 8% capping weight (as opposed to say 5%) means the top 10 stocks will likely take up a huge chunk of the portfolio (they currently account for 62% of the total portfolio), making this very top-heavy.

Even though we have an 8% cap, note that ESPO’s tracking index is still primarily weighted on the basis of these stocks’ individual market-caps, so even though one gets access to small-caps (the additional liquidity and volume screeners will largely eradicate micro-caps) tilt towards giant and large-caps which make up 58% of the portfolio in aggregate. ESPO’s average market-cap of $27B also puts it in the large-cap territory.

This is supposed to be a global portfolio and one gets access to stocks from 9 different countries, with stocks from two countries in particular- Japan and US dominating with weights of 28% each.

As far as traditional sector classification is concerned, the bulk of the holdings come from the communication sector, with rest comprising of discretionary and tech sector stocks. What’s common between all three are they are high-beta sectors with strong growth characteristics.

Speaking of growth (stocks with above average sales and earnings growth, strong momentum, relatively pricey valuations, etc.) and other styles, the former cohort does enjoy a useful stake accounting for 42% of the portfolio, but at the end of the day, hybrid stocks still dominate with a 55% weight (stocks with both growth and value characteristics).

Risks Associated With ESPO

One could argue that ESPO doesn’t do a great job of fully replicating its benchmark index. We say this because the tracking errors shown in the image below (across different durations) are roughly twice as much as what other ETFs experience, and for a narrow portfolio of 25-27 stocks that isn’t good enough.

Elevated tracking errors aren’t the only concern; even the variability of ESPO’s returns tend to be quite widely divergent from its average returns and that is captured by above-average standard deviation and annualized volatility readings. This makes ESPO quite an unideal product to utilize as a core holding in one’s portfolio.

ESPO is also not particularly cost-efficient, priced at an net expense ratio of 0.56% that is more than the median expense ratio of ETFs. Also take note of ESPO’s wide differential between its bid and its ask (in percentage terms it is almost twice as much as what other ETFs experience). Products with such wide bid-asks tend to cause slippage risks (the risk of entering and exiting positions at prices that are quite different from what was initially budgeted for).

Investors should also consider that a good chunk of this portfolio (close to two-thirds) remains vulnerable to foreign currency depreciation versus the USD. ESPO may be a USD denominated product, but only 35% of its holdings are denominated in USD terms with the rest being denominated in global currencies particularly the JPY and the HKD. Thus, even if those foreign stocks do well in their local currencies, if those currencies end up depreciating against the dollar during the same reporting period, investors of ESPO could end up taking a hit on their net returns.

Prima facie, since ESPO is supposed to cover global stocks, it may give investors the false illusion of being a solid diversifier away from the US markets. But that isn’t necessarily true, and it tends to move in the same direction as the benchmark equity index.

This can be gleaned from a positive and moderately strong correlation of 0.65x, and a relatively high beta co-efficient of 0.75x (for every $1 movement in the S&P500, ESPO typically follows by $0.75.

ESPO’s valuations too are hardly compelling, trading roughly on par with the S&P500 on a price-to-earnings basis, and trading at a steeper premium of 19% on a price-to-cashflow basis.

The relatively pricier cash flow multiple also implies that ESPO’s holdings don’t have a meaningful enough free cash flow [FCF)) base that is widely seen as a mark of quality. One could argue that the lack of meaningful implied FCF is also because these gaming and eSports businesses are devoting a lot of resources to R&D [Research & Development] which often includes a lot of speculative bets that tend to result in sub-par outcomes or project that are eventually shelved.

Who Is ESPO ETF For?

We noted earlier how ESPO’s P/E valuations don’t look meaningfully cheap to the S&P500, but do also consider that for that roughly similar P/E multiple, ESPO’s holdings are staring at a much more attractive runway of earnings growth, which is also much better than the Nasdaq

Put another way, as we implied earlier (hybrid stocks), ESPO has the look of a product that would appeal to GARP (Growth-at-a-reasonable-price) type investors.

Contrary to popular belief, video gaming is also no longer a niche terrain that only appeals to the youngsters of this world. Infact the addressable market is now becoming a lot broader with even a wider proportion of Millennials and Boomers taking to it.

With E-sports the market is still relatively nascent, and the growth potential is even more enormous with the global market poised to almost triple by the end of this decade.

Basically, by getting onboard with ESPO you are getting access to a rapidly-expanding and increasingly popular entertainment terrain that is spreading its tentacles across vast swathes of the global population as time progresses.

Then, if you’re someone who wants to get hold of industries where AI penetration is progressing at a rapid pace, and leading to strong efficiencies, this product works as a great theme. AI is particularly deeply entrenched in game development (see image below), but you’re also seeing that in pockets such as game execution, and monetization as well.

What ETF Alternatives Are There Besides ESPO?

ESPO is by far the largest ETF in the video-gaming and e-sports terrain, but if investors are not happy with ESPO’s qualities and want some lesser-known alternatives, they may also consider the Global X Video Games & Esports ETF (HERO) or the NERD Video Games ETF (NERD) which also happen to be more cost-efficient and offer far superior yields than our product in focus.

HERO, which got listed a year after ESPO, has so far amassed AUM of $120M, and passively tracks an index called the Solactive Video Games & Esports Index. It offers the widest reach (48 stocks, almost double of ESPO’s coverage), with some useful micro-cap exposure, and an average market-cap that is closer to mid-cap territory. HERO is also the only ETF of the three which pays dividends twice a year. However, what we find very concerning with HERO is the inordinately wide bid-asks associated with it, despite having better AUM and daily liquidity than NERD

NERD stands out because it is not passively managed like the other two but has an active manager picking stocks, with a greater tilt towards Japanese stocks and the communication services sector that the other two portfolios. Despite being actively managed, it is also the most stable portfolio of the three with the lowest annual turnover ratio. Top-heaviness seems to be an issue here as well, despite covering a much wider pool of stocks than our product in focus. However, it also offers a yield that is 4x better than ESPO.

Having said all this, investors may still want to note that when it comes to delivering the goods (so far), ESPO has comfortably outperformed the other two portfolios since NERD (the youngest product of the three) got listed.

Summary

ESPO is a focused, growth-oriented, global ETF for investors who want to gain access to a market category (Video game and e-sports) that is seeing tremendous traction across the world. However, ESPO may not offer the most cost-efficient exposure to this space, yields an unremarkable figure, and like most alternatives in this space, is also likely to be susceptible to slippage risk.

This article answers these three main questions about ESPO:

  1. What are the main characteristics of the ESPO portfolio?
  2. What are the major risks associated with ESPO, and what type of investor should use this product?
  3. How does ESPO compare to other ETFs in the gaming and eSport space?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Open Text Continues Transition But Growth Challenges Remain
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Open Text Corporation (OTEX) reported its FQ1 2026 financial results, beating both revenue and earnings estimates.

I previously analyzed OTEX in April 2025 with a Hold outlook due to numerous uncertainties amid a strategic shift toward subscriptions, AI, and security solutions.

The firm continues to make progress on its transformation as it sheds non-core assets, reduces debt and continues share repurchases.

I remain a neutral Hold on OTEX as we watch its transition and capital allocation remains shareholder-friendly.

Open Text’s Market And Approach

OpenText operates in various information management markets globally and has recently been divesting itself of certain non-core business segments.

The firm’s main offerings are:

  • Analytics

  • B2B collaboration

  • AI and ML solutions

  • Content management

  • Cybersecurity cloud

  • Customer experience cloud

  • DevOps cloud

  • Observability & monitoring

  • API and Integrations.

Per a 2025 market research report by Grand View Research, the global information management market was around $110 billion in 2024 and was forecasted to reach $221 billion by 2030.

If achieved, this growth rate would represent a CAGR of about 12.4% from 2025 to 2030.

The primary reasons for this projected growth are the growing usage of data in increasingly complex environments amid continuously increasing information management and security requirements.

The graphic here shows the historical and projected future growth path of the worldwide enterprise data management market from 2020 through 2030:

Major competitors and other industry participants include:

  • Oracle (ORCL)

  • IBM (IBM)

  • Teradata (TDC)

  • SAP SE (SAP)

  • Teradata (TDC)

  • Amazon Web Services (AMZN)

  • Cloudera

  • MindTree Ltd.

  • Broadcom (AVGO)

  • Hyland

  • Box (BOX)

  • Dropbox (DBX)

  • Laserfiche

  • M-files

  • Informatica (Salesforce).

The company seeks new customers through its direct sales and business development efforts and via technology partners, solution extension partners and strategic partners.

OTEX derives most of its revenue from the two segments from Customer Support and Cloud Services and Subscriptions, and by region, from the Americas, as the pie charts show here:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary market characteristics:

Strengths

  • Well-known brand in information management, comprehensive offerings, large B2B network.

Weaknesses

  • Portfolio sprawl, perception of a "legacy" operator, heavy debt load.

Opportunities

  • Generative AI “Aviator,” migrating on-prem clients to cloud, consolidated security portfolio growth, middle-market expansion.

Threats

  • Cloud-native competition, declining revenue from divestitures, geopolitical volatility.

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has slightly declined sequentially from soft maintenance and license revenue, although it rose 1.5% YoY from strength in Cloud and License revenue. Operating income by quarter (red line) has risen due to sequentially lower SG&A and R&D expenses outpacing the revenue drop.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has remained stable with no discernible trend. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have varied within a range, while R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have moved lower as the company has just completed a major product cycle.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have fluctuated with no meaningful trend due in part to seasonalities, as the chart shows here:

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, OTEX ended the quarter with around $1.1 billion in cash and equivalents and $6.4 billion in total debt, of which $35.9 million was due within twelve months.

In the past 12 months, OTEX’s stock price has risen by 14% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Technology-Software ETF’s (IGV) gain of only 1%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a helpful financial and operating metrics table for OTEX, including forward and trailing twelve-month figures:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

Hold: 3.13

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

The company's Rule of 40 performance has improved markedly in recent quarters:

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $1.2 billion, and capital expenditures were $150.4 million.

The company paid $93.0 million in stock-based compensation, or SBC, in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $1.1 billion.

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $504.8 million, as the company has been divesting non-core assets and repurchasing stock with some of the proceeds.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was just 1.8%, a reasonable figure for a company of Open Text’s maturity.

Management’s plans for capital allocation in relation to shares are to continue to consistently repurchase shares alongside debt reduction and dividend distributions.

Overall, Open Text is following a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach as it divests non-core units while keeping SBC and CapEx expenses in check.

Why I’m Neutral On Open Text

Open Text is still in the midst of a transition as it slims down and increases its focus on AI, security and related offerings.

The company intends to divest non-core units representing 15% to 20% of total revenue when completed.

Management’s current business and growth strategy involves focusing on its AI-led approach through the firm’s Aviator platform, unifying its fragmented product offerings through its Titanium X modernization efforts and expansion to selling to the developer ecosystem rather than just CIOs.

Revenue growth, although low year-over-year, exceeded expectations and the firm closed a substantial number of large deals in Q3 2025, growing YoY in new deals greater than $1 million in ACV.

Also, its cloud RPO (Remaining Performance Obligations) moved up 11% YoY, with enterprise cloud RPO up 20% YoY, signals that customers are interested in its offerings.

The graphic below shows the frequency of various negative keywords in the most recent earnings conference call between management and analysts:

The graphic indicates that the company is continuing to experience declining demand from on-prem solutions and related maintenance services.

I’ve compared Open Text to a large basket of public information technology companies as compiled by noted valuation expert Dr. Aswath Damodaran in January 2025:

NYU Stern / Damodaran Basket

Industry Name:

Information Services

Metric

(Glossary Of Terms.)

(Source - NYU Stern School & Dr. Aswath Damodaran.)

The table suggests that OTEX is producing significantly higher net margin despite receiving a lower EV/EBITDA multiple and Price/Sales multiple, at least as of the January 2025 basket comparison date.

Looking ahead, management believes that its Cloud Content business will "soon be the dominant share of all" of its revenue sources.

OTEX is pivoting toward an agentic and GenAI focus, believing that it is well-placed to help companies increase their productivity through training their AI systems on their own proprietary data sources.

The difficulty that OTEX and its clients will face is the inherently probabilistic nature of these new generative AI systems, whereas companies need deterministic responses that are repeatable every time in the same way.

This issue of probabilistic AI versus deterministic business and operating requirements is a critical one for companies large and small, and is likely restraining more companies from going live to production with their initial tests and pilot programs.

In any event, I expect OTEX to continue to divest more non-core units at around one deal per quarter, paying down debt and returning funds to shareholders in various ways as it continues its transition.

The near-term risk is that organic revenue growth is low in 2026 and earnings and cash flow continue to tread water, leading to a stalled stock.

However, OTEX valuation appears to be reasonable given the company's shifting business focus, even after the stock’s performance so far in 2025, so my outlook on OTEX remains a Hold as we watch the firm’s transformation.

data.title: Cathay General Bancorp: Still Cheap Amid Ongoing NIM Expansion
data.description:
Cathay General Bancorp (CATY) has been ticking along nicely in recent months. As I'd hoped when I first covered this California-based lender, Cathay's net interest margin has continued to expand back towards its historical average, driving decent earnings growth for the firm. Shares have also clocked in a reasonable performance. They're up around 6% in that time (with dividends) - not bad for a 5-month period, and nearly double the return of the wider regional bank space.

Looking back, I did think Cathay was quite cheap in my opening piece. Shares were trading at a discount to their historical book value and earnings multiples at the time, and this remains the case today. Granted, there are some "buts" to consider here, although I think Cathay's valuation and growth prospects outweigh them.

Recovery-Driven Growth

To quickly recap, Cathay's net interest margin ('NIM') was hit pretty hard during the final stages of the Fed's tightening cycle. Having averaged 3.45% in the decade prior, it fell to just 3.04% in 2024.

There were a couple of reasons for this. Firstly, Cathay's funding base isn't particularly great. Time balances were nearly 50% of its deposits last quarter, while more lucrative non-interest-bearing balances were less than 20% of the mix.

Secondly, since Cathay operates with a relatively high loan-to-deposit ratio (nearly 100% per below), it can't be too aggressive in allowing its higher-cost funding to roll off. Said differently, it needs to be prepared to pay up to retain its deposits.

Because there aren't really any incremental expenses associated with NIM-driven changes to revenue, this development had a particularly large impact on Cathay's earnings. Last year, its net income declined by 20%.

The good news is that the bank is now benefiting from the reverse situation: interest rates are falling, and given the nature of its market-like funding base, this is largely flowing through to Cathay's interest costs. With that, Cathay reported a 3.28% cost of interest-bearing deposits last quarter, down 67bps year-on-year.

Turning to the other side of its balance sheet, Cathay's loan book skews around 60% to fixed-rate loans. A portion of these assets were put on its book before rates rose, and the upwards repricing of these maturities is helping to keep Cathay's asset yield relatively buoyant. While its blended loan yield did fall 21bps year-on-year last quarter to 6.14%, this was clearly much less than the decline in its funding costs.

That dynamic has seen Cathay deliver solid NIM expansion and earnings growth recently. At 3.31%, the former was up 27bps year-on-year last quarter, helping to power 12% growth in net interest income. Earnings per share rose around 20% to $1.13, benefiting from the high degree of operating leverage associated with NIM-driven revenue growth.

Shares Still Look Cheap

Following its third-quarter performance, Cathay has now earned $4.33 per share in trailing-four-quarter net income. Shares trade for $49.61 as I type, so they're trading for around 11.5x this figure. As you can see, that is pretty close to their ten-year average multiple (~11.9x).

While this would appear to suggest that Cathay is fairly valued, its recovery-driven earnings growth is far from done. Per the recent earnings call, management thinks that its NIM can carry on expanding as short-term interest rates fall:

And we think just based on what happened in 2025 that for every 0.25 point drop in Fed funds over 6 months, our NIM should go up 6 or 7 basis points. So with another rate cut possible here in October or certainly for sure in December and then probably 1 or 2 more in '26, we think directionally our NIM will go up or should go up.

Heng Chen, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer

If so, then the associated operating leverage should continue to power decent earnings growth. Analysts appear to agree. Consensus has Cathay's quarterly EPS rising to over $1.30 by the end of next year, around 17% higher than last quarter's print.

This would also leave the stock at a more modest multiple of 9.7x estimated 2026 EPS. If Cathay were to hold its current trailing P/E ratio, then its shares could be in the high-$50s area in just over 12 months. Cathay's $0.34 quarterly dividend would lift this to a circa $60 total return price, which is roughly 20% above the prevailing quote. In other words, there's a margin of safety should earnings come in below expectations - or if Cathay's P/E were to trade below its long-run average.

Other valuation measures also look attractive. For example, Cathay reported $36.96 per share in tangible book value at the end of last quarter, for a multiple of around 1.35x. Per the chart above, this represents a double-digit discount to its long-run average.

Addressing Credit Risk

I said in the introduction that there were some "buts" to consider here, and I was thinking chiefly about credit risk. First, it's important to appreciate that Cathay's asset concentration skews heavily to loans. Loans accounted for around 84% of its assets last quarter, which is nearly 20 points higher than the average bank with over $10 billion in total assets. This means Cathay's earnings will be particularly sensitive to credit quality trends.

Within its loan book, there's particularly large exposure to non-owner occupied commercial real estate. Cathay had over $8 billion in non-owner-occupied CRE loans as of last quarter, mapping to a ratio of nearly 300% relative to risk-based capital. That's a much higher concentration ratio than the average bank.

Here are a few counterpoints that may assuage these concerns. Firstly, Cathay's profitability has been improving alongside its NIM. It generated a 2.26% pre-provision return on its assets last quarter, so it can absorb a relatively high rate of credit losses before it becomes loss making.

Secondly, collateral cover in Cathay's CRE book looks healthy. Including owner-occupied loans, Cathay's blended loan-to-value ratio is less than 50%, and just 1% of its CRE loans had an LTV above 70%. Should defaults rise, well, Cathay has a large cushion before these would turn into actual losses. Related to that, Cathay has already set aside the equivalent of 0.93% of its loans in its loss reserve.

Finally, Cathay has ample capital. The bank disclosed a 13.15% ratio of CET1 capital to risk-weighted assets last quarter, easily exceeding its effective regulatory minimum (7%). In dollar terms, that gives Cathay a circa $1.2 billion cushion, equal to around 6% of its loan book.

Conclusion

Given its asset concentration and CRE exposure, I can see why the market is still adopting a cautious stance toward Cathay General Bancorp. However, between improving pre-provision profitability, healthy collateral cover in its CRE book, and robust capital, I think Cathay has a strong answer to any credit concerns. With prospects for earnings growth and valuation also looking attractive, I remain bullish. Accordingly, I am reiterating my "Buy" rating.

data.title: KNG: Efficient Dividend Fund But Underperforms Peers (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When I first started building my dividend portfolio, I opted for dividend aristocrats because these were companies that could help build a strong foundation of stability and consistent dividends. FT Cboe Vest S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF (KNG) exposes investors to dividend aristocrats but takes it a step further by writing options against its holdings to amplify the income level. When I previously covered KNG, I issued a buy rating due to the fund's resilience and ability to preserve capital during periods of uncertainty. However, I wanted to revisit the fund to compare it against some notable high-yield peers out there.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that KNG's share price has declined by about 9.25%. The fund has essentially failed to participate in the growth of the market over the last few months. Even when including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at a loss of 1.13% over the same time frame. KNG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of approximately 8.7% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. While KNG may be an enticing choice for income investors, I believe there are some drawbacks to the fund's performance.

Despite the high dividend yield and fund's solid historical performance, I believe that there are some more compelling peers for investors to choose from. As the demand for income funds have increased, firms are starting to perfect the strategies used to generate a high income, while also allowing the fund to participate in the growth of the market. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that KNG implements to generate its income for its shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, KNG has total net assets of $3.4B that is spread across 138 different holdings. The fund aims to track the performance of the Cboe S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index, so the fund will allocate at least 80% of its assets into common stocks within the index. In order to generate the income needed, KNG also implements an option writing strategy against a portion of its holdings. This allows KNG to capture option premiums, which are then distributed as a dividend.

When looking at the breakdown of the fund, we can see that KNG is most heavily allocated to Consumer Staples at 22.47% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Industrials and Financials, which accounts for 22.25% and 12.85% of assets respectively. Since the fund is focused on stocks that have maintained at least 25 years of consistent dividend raises, the fund doesn't have much exposure to Technology stocks, since technology companies aren't typically known for their ability to raise dividends over time.

KNG implements an ATM (at-the-money) option writing strategy against 21.61% of its assets. According to the fund's overview, this allows KNG to participate in 78.39% of the potential upside of its holdings. However, the ATM option strategy means that the selected strike price is set very close to the market price of the underlying asset, which limits the upside growth that can be captured in the first place. So if an underlying stock is trading at $99 per share, KNG may write an option with a strike price at $100 per share, which only captures a small upside but also generates a premium.

When looking at the holdings of the fund, we can see that KNG is exposed to many high quality companies. For instance, Albemarle Corp (ALB) is the largest holding at 2.03% of assets. This is followed by many other notable holdings such as Cardinal Health (CAH), C.H Robinson Worldwide (CHRW), Expeditors International of Washington (EXPD), and Nucor Corp (NUE), just to name a few.

Performance Insights & Comparison

Despite KNG providing exposure to so many high quality businesses, the inclusion of an option strategy means that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation. To demonstrate this, we can refer to the fund's performance since inception below. As we can see, KNG's share price has increased by about 23.6%. When including all distributions, the total return jumps up to 88.32% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

While the fund has demonstrated its ability to be an efficient long-term holding, there are some peers that offer a compelling value proposition. While their underlying holdings, sector exposure, dividend yield, and option writing strategy may differ, the underlying premise of generating income from a portfolio of equities remains similar. I believe that two of the most compelling peers are the following funds:

  • Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO): ~9.8% dividend yield.
  • Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX): ~8% dividend yield.

As we can see below, KNG is the largest in terms of assets under management but it also has the highest expense ratio. Conversely, QDVO has the shortest operating history and the lowest level of assets under management. However, QDVO also has the highest dividend yield of the group. GPIX is the middle of the road choice here with the lowest dividend yield but also the most cost-efficient option.

The standout difference here is that both QDVO and GPIX implement dynamic option writing strategies, where management shifts the frequency in which it writes options based on market conditions. So when markets are rapidly rising, QDVO and GPIX may write fewer options so that they can better participate in the growth of the market. Conversely, these peers may write more options during periods of high volatility, which can offset declines and lead to higher distributions.

When comparing the performance of these peers, we can see that KNG severely underperforms. Despite the limited operating window, we can still see that KNG's underlying strategy, holding breakdown, and option writing strategy may not be as efficient compared to these peers. In comparison, QDVO's total return significantly outpaces the peers at 34.79%. Even from a price growth perspective, QDVO demonstrates its ability to participate in market rallies better than these peers.

Additionally, KNG's limited exposure to technology companies ultimately results in underperformance. The focus on dividend aristocrats may result in better price stability during periods of uncertainty, but this aligns investors for underperformance during market rallies. In a scenario where the growth of the market continues to be led by AI, KNG may be aligned to continue underperforming over the next few years.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.3439 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.7%. Since payouts are influenced by the success of KNG's written options, implied volatility levels, and the momentum of the market, the distribution amounts can slightly vary every month. As we can see below, payouts consistently shifted but have remained in a very close range. Therefore, KNG may not be a great choice for investors that are looking for an easy to predict income on a monthly basis.

Additionally, KNG's structure may not allow the fund to produce consistent dividend increases over time. So despite the exposure to holdings that are known to put an emphasis on dividend raises, this may not translate to KNG paying higher distributions over time.

On a more positive note, KNG has a history of paying out distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 97.83% of the distributions on a YTD basis were paid out with return of capital. Unlike net investment income, return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

This means that KNG can be efficiently utilized within a taxable brokerage account with minimal tax implications. However, a small portion of the distributions are still paid out using net investment income and short-term capital gains, which may still result in certain tax consequences. So despite the majority of the distributions being paid out in a tax-efficient manner, this doesn't automatically mean that dividends are tax free.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on KNG to a hold at this time. While the fund gets the job done and it offers a higher yield than some peers, the fund severely underperforms more compelling peers that are capable of better participating in the growth of the market. KNG's ATM option strategy ultimately caps the upside price movement and this can lead to underperformance through bull markets. Additionally, KNG's focus on dividend aristocrats means that the fund is lacking a meaningful allocation to technology stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Zuckerberg To Metaverse: It's Not Me, It's You
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Meta looking to make big cuts to Metaverse team as it focuses on AI. (0:15) Is AI’s future now in Paris? (1:18) Is Bugs Bunny destined for Stranger Things? (2:55)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Metaverse, we hardly knew ye.

Meta Platforms (META) shares are rallying after Bloomberg reported that CEO Mark Zuckerberg is preparing to make sharp cuts to the company’s metaverse operations.

Executives have discussed budget reductions of up to 30% next year for the group that includes Meta Horizon Worlds and its Quest virtual reality unit — a move that would likely lead to layoffs as soon as January.

The bulk of the cuts would hit Meta’s virtual reality group, which accounts for most of the company’s metaverse-related spending, though no final decisions have been made.

The proposed reductions are part of Meta’s 2026 budget planning, which included a series of meetings at Zuckerberg’s Hawaii compound last month, Bloomberg said.

Zuckerberg has asked Meta executives to look for 10% cuts across the board, consistent with recent budget cycles, but the metaverse unit was asked to cut deeper this year because the technology has not generated the level of industry-wide competition the company expected.

The metaverse effort has drawn scrutiny from investors, who view it as a drain on resources, and from watchdogs, who have raised concerns about children’s privacy and safety in virtual environments.

And as Zuckerberg moves from the Metaverse to the land of AI, his AI guru is looking at a manifest destiny of AI from California to France.

Like the AI trade? Brush up your French.

Yann LeCun, the renowned artificial intelligence scientist who has an entire page devoted to how to pronounce his name and is leaving Meta to start a new AI company, said his current employer will not invest in his startup.

The unnamed company may be based outside of Silicon Valley, which is “completely hypnotized by generative models,” LeCun said at the AI-Pulse event.

“Our best AI systems can pass the bar exam, write code. But we still don’t have a robot that can do what a five-year-old can. We are missing something big.”

You “have to do this kind of work outside the Valley, in Paris.”

Among active stocks, Salesforce (CRM) impressed with results and guidance, giving shares a small boost.

Morgan Stanley analyst Keith Weiss said a bigger beat and slight acceleration in current remaining performance obligation, coupled with an impressive ramp in Agentforce metrics, reinforces “the theme of building momentum we heard at the Analyst Day.”

Kroger (KR) was under pressure as identical-store sales ex fuel rose 2.6%, missing expectations of 2.9%. Looking ahead, Kroger sees full-year sales comps of 2.8% to 3.0%. Full-year EPS of $4.75 to $4.80 (midpoint $4.775) a tad below the $4.79 consensus.

Wedbush Securities said C3.ai’s (AI) better-than-expected quarterly results are seen as a “good step in the right direction” following recent turbulence, including a management shakeup. Analysts aid new CEO Stephen Ehikian is on the right path, but that the enterprise software company “needs to show consistency.”

In other news of note, Netflix (NFLX) is the leading bidder for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), according to a CNBC report.

Netflix is bidding 85% in cash and 15% in stock for Warner, according to the report, with bidding likely to end early next week

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Apollo Global Management Chief Economist Torsten Slok has identified five downside scenarios to watch next year.

First, the U.S. economy could re-accelerate, driven by the fading trade-war shock and the One Big Beautiful Bill, pushing inflation higher from already elevated levels.

Second, a global industrial renaissance could boost growth as more countries homeshore advanced manufacturing, and ramp investments in infrastructure, energy, defense, and supply chains.

Third, a new Fed Chair could cut rates for political reasons, regardless of inflation dynamics or labor-market conditions.

Fourth, an AI bubble bursting could trigger a major correction in the Magnificent Seven, slowing capex and high-end consumer spending.

And fifth, a sharp increase in fixed-income supply in 2026 — from rising government deficits and hyperscaler issuance — could push rates and credit spreads higher, impacting bonds and credit markets

data.title: VOT: Offering Diversified Mid-Cap Growth Exposure
data.description:
Fast Facts

Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (VOT) was launched on 08/17/2006 and “is designed to accurately represent the mid-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity market and deliver low turnover.” It has a portfolio of 121 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.60% and an expense ratio of 0.07%. It is a very large and liquid ETF, with $33 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $49.5 million. The underlying index, CRSP US Mid Cap Growth Index, is also available reflected in a Vanguard in a mutual fund (VMGMX). Vanguard Group was founded in 1975, is based in Malvern, Pennsylvania, and has about $11 trillion in total AUM. Vanguard has a unique ownership structure: it is owned by its U.S.-based funds, which are owned by their shareholders.

Strategy

Limited information is disclosed about the methodology. According to the index provider CRSP (Center for Research in Security Prices, an affiliate of the University of Chicago), “CRSP Market Indexes are based on a proprietary methodology using publicly available market data.” CRSP growth indexes represent “70% to 85% of cumulative capitalization of CRSP US Total Market”. The fund’s turnover rate was 21% in the most recent fiscal year and 14% in the previous year. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index CRSP US Mid Cap Index, represented by Vanguard Mid-Cap ETF (VO).

VOT Portfolio

The fund is almost exclusively invested in U.S.-domiciled companies (98% of asset value), with quite balanced exposure in industrials (22.7%), technology (20.7%) and consumer discretionary (19.1%). Other sectors are below 12%. Compared to the parent index, VOT overweights the top three sectors and healthcare, while it downplays mostly utilities, energy, consumer staples and basic materials.

The fund is well-diversified, with low company risk. As an example, the top 10 holdings from 12/4/2025, listed in the next table, represent 21.7% of asset value and the top name weighs 3.05% (the list may have changed by the time you read this).

Performance

Although VOT has a low yield and is not designed to attract dividend investors, it boasts strong dividend growth. The annual sum of distributions has increased by 110% between 2014 and 2024, greatly outpacing cumulative inflation (about 34% in the same time, based on CPI).

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of VOT and five of the largest mid-cap growth ETFs:

VOT is the largest and most liquid of these funds. It has the second-lowest expense ratio (almost on par with IMCG) and the lowest turnover. It comes in third position for total return since December 2016, and second behind IMCG in Sharpe ratio.

Takeaway

Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (VOT) has a portfolio of 121 mid-size growth companies, well diversified across holdings and sectors. VOT has outperformed a mid-cap benchmark since its inception and a number of mid-cap growth ETFs over the last nine years, although IMCG has a better track record. VOT is well-suited for long-term investors seeking diversified exposure to the growth factor besides the usual mega-caps. It may also be part of a rotational strategy involving an array of ETFs focused on various investing styles, and periodically selecting holdings based on market conditions and relative strength (tactical allocation).

  • Pros: low fees, high liquidity, low company risk, sector diversification, outperforms its parent index and some peers.
  • Cons: maybe not the best in its category.

This article answers these three main questions about VOT:

  1. How is VOT's portfolio structured, and how does it compare to its benchmark?
  2. How does VOT compare to its peers?
  3. What type of strategy is VOT best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: nCino: Growth Slowdown Offsets Cheap Valuation (Downgrade)
data.description:
The stock market in 2025 has been clearly divided into a concentrated camp of large-cap growth winners, and all the rest of the small and mid-cap value stocks that have seen sharp corrections this year. As we move into 2026, my focus is on rotating more of my portfolio into small and mid-cap names that have their own catalysts to outperform the S&P 500 (SP500).

As a result of the broad underperformance in small and mid-cap stocks, we have a lot of bargain options to choose from. And that means we can afford to be picky: and I'm chasing stocks that have high fundamental quality as well as a cheap price. nCino, Inc. (NCNO) may be cheap, but its decelerating growth no. longer offers the quality that I'm after. Down more than 20% this year, and falling modestly after a recent disappointing Q3 earnings print, I think nCino's opportunity for an upward re-rating will be marred by its sluggish growth.

I last wrote a Buy rating on nCino in September, when the stock was trading just shy of $28 per share. Since then, nCino has slid ~10% and underperformed the S&P 500. I'm losing patience with this trade, especially with nCino's slowing growth, and I'm resetting the stock back down to a neutral rating. nCino's relatively cheap valuation is likely to prevent the company from substantial downside, but at the same time I don't see any meaningful growth catalysts that can help the stock expand multiples from current lows.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for nCino. On the bright side for this company, I still see the following tailwinds:

  • nCino addresses a large $19.5 billion SAM, and has substantial overseas growth potential. The company estimates its serviceable addressable market ((SAM)) at $19.5 billion, which indicates that its current revenue run rate (just over $0.5 billion) is only less than 3% penetrated into its overall market. The company is also beginning an international growth push, recently landing large Spanish and Japanese financial institutions to its customer base.
  • Recurring revenue and blue-chip customers. nCino enjoys a recurring software revenue base, with blue-chip financial services customers including the likes of Wells Fargo (WFC), Barclays, Bank of America (BAC), and U.S. Bancorp (USB).

With this in mind, however, a number of risks have also cropped up for the company:

  • Choppy trends in nCino's mortgage business. nCino is positioning its mortgage banking software product, which is currently a mid-teens percentage of overall revenue, as one of its key growth drivers. However, recent growth rates have been choppy, suggesting that the product may not be gaining immense traction.
  • Potential AI disruption. nCino doesn't enjoy any AI tailwinds. Many software companies have fallen sharply this year on fears that foundational AI models may create enterprise software tools that encroach on their territory. As more banks begin to invest in automation and AI, they may end up bypassing the nCino software platform.

nCino's valuation remains cheap, but with so many good options now to invest in the software industry, its bargain multiples are no longer compelling enough for me to stay invested in this trade and wait patiently for growth to rebound. At current share prices near $25, nCino trades at a market cap of $2.96 billion. After we net off the $130.2 million of cash and $218.9 million of debt on nCino's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $3.05 billion.

For the remainder of FY26 (which ends in January 2026 for nCino), the company has guided to $591.9-$593.4 million in revenue, or 9-10% y/y growth, up from 8-9% previously.

And for next year FY27, Wall Street is expecting the company to generate $1.01 of pro forma EPS (+19% y/y) on $640.0 million in revenue (+8% y/y). This positions nCino's valuation multiples at

  • 5.1x EV/FY26 revenue and 4.8x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 27x FY26 P/E and 25x FY27 P/E.

These are reasonably low multiples, but we should consider the fact that the entire software industry (save for AI-boosted plays like Oracle (ORCL) and Palantir (PLTR)) has taken a deep leg lower this year on AI competition fears. nCino is no longer a bargain stock when we stack it up against other penalty-box software names like Workday (WDAY) and Salesforce (CRM), which arguably have better innovation in AI to claim and more growth catalysts than nCino.

In my view, nCino is likely to trade in a range-bound pattern through next year, and I'd rather buy the dip in a more proven, higher-recognition software stock like Atlassian (TEAM) or HubSpot (HUBS) rather than spend my patience on nCino. Lock in losses here and move on to invest elsewhere.

Q3 download

Let's now run through nCino's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

nCino's revenue grew 10% y/y to $152.2 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $147.4 million (+6% y/y). However, we note that revenue growth decelerated two points relative to 12% y/y growth in Q2.

Previously, I had high hopes for the accelerating trend in nCino's mortgage business, which is currently ~18% of overall subscription revenue. And yet in Q3, mortgage subscription revenue growth of $21 million decelerated to 2% y/y, after accelerating for two straight quarters.

It appears now that the mortgage product is more likely to be a slower-burning, run rate type of product than an expansive new sales opportunity for nCino. There's still potential upside here if rates fall and mortgage activity heightens, which may spur more banks to arm their mortgage divisions with software tools.

Still, I'm more in the cautious camp on this company now. This is especially true as the banking sector faces potential headwinds, particularly among smaller regional banks: the market is now fearing a credit crisis after a series of high-profile credit defaults. None of this is good news for a company that focuses on the financial services vertical.

The company insists that business is healthy and that it is seeing good pipeline progression. Per CFO Gregory Orenstein's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

In terms of the sales activity we're seeing on a global basis, we feel good. We feel good about the business right now.

And the pipeline, I think as we sit here and talk today, we feel incrementally better than the last time we spoke with you. And so we have to continue to execute. That's the drum we continue to beat here. We saw the team respond incredibly well in the third quarter, and we expect that to continue to be the focus is just execution. But the business is out there. And again, I think the story is resonating.

I think what we're doing from an AI standpoint, leveraging the customers that we have and the innovation that this company has always been front and center on really is resonating right now in the marketplace."

However, one point of concern that management disclosed was that nCino lost a competitive deal in Q3, which caused a one-time contract buyout worth $0.5 million (contributing to ~40bps of the company's revenue growth in Q3). This may be a fresh reminder that nCino's products may not be very sticky upon renewal.

On the profitability front, we note that the company has continued to drive economies of scale, pushing pro forma operating margins up 6 points y/y to 26%.

Pro forma EPS of $0.31 grew 55% y/y and beat Street expectations of $0.20, but we note that the stock isn't yet generating sufficient EPS to fully justify its valuation, with P/E multiples still sitting above key software industry peers.

Key takeaways

The stocks in my portfolio for 2026 need to be cheap and of high quality, and nCino is no longer growing at a pace that meets the quality test for me. The stock is looking more and more like a value trap that will be hard-pressed to convince the markets to rerate it upward. Steer clear here.

data.title: Air France-KLM: Mixed Q3, 2026 Visibility Still Cloudy
data.description:
In light of the Q3 results and Air France-KLM’s (OTCPK:AFRAF; OTCPK:AFLYY) latest announcement, we are returning with fresh commentary on the company. It was a mixed quarter, but our 2026 view is unchanged; with the stock near our €11.5 target, we maintain a Neutral rating (Fig. 1)

Our view was supported by 1) ex-fuel costs are still trending higher as industry demand softens, 2) no guidance uplift, 3) the KLM segment continues to struggle, and 4) a valuation that leaves limited upside.

Fig 1

Q3 Results and Our Neutral View

In Q3, the company welcomed 29.2 million passengers, a 4.7% increase over last year. Top-line sales increased by 2.6% to €9.2 billion (Fig. 2). These positive results were driven by passengers and Air France-KLM’s maintenance segment, partly offset by Cargo. The company's capacity increased by 5.1% and the load factor decreased to 88.8% from 89.3%. Group RASK fell 0.5% year over year at constant currency, weighed by softer unit revenues from Cargo (-5.1%) and Transavia (-2.8%). The passenger network improved 0.5%, supported by premium cabins and long-haul. Going down to the P&L report, Air France-KLM reached €1.2 billion with stable results compared to last year. This represents a 13.1% margin, supported by lower fuel prices and offset by unit costs up 1.3% and a 0.5% decline in unit revenues. As reported, the solidarity tax on tickets and Schiphol has higher costs, significantly impacting costs. The company recorded a 41% increase in Air Traffic Control and airport charges. We also need to consider that last year the Olympic Games had a positive impact.

Looking at the cash flow, the company recorded a positive FCF of €700 million, and leverage was down with a current EBITDA/debt ratio of 1.6x. Therefore, the Air France-KLM 2025 outlook was reconfirmed (Fig. 4).

Source: Air France-KLM Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still neutral?

OBR projections point to higher air passenger duty and the rollout of tourist taxes on overnight stays. These new taxes are spread across Europe, with the latest example in Milan ahead of the upcoming Winter Olympics. These measures could lead to lower travel demand and weigh on airline players (hotel operators and related services).

Separately, planned airport tariff increases, such as the Schiphol case in Q3 2025, may divert traffic to alternative airports, likely favoring low-cost carriers such as Eurowings (from Deutsche Lufthansa AG), Ryanair, and easyJet.

In the Q3 results, Air France-KLM’s operating results were roughly ~5% below Visible Alpha consensus (with a larger miss at the net income level). The €23 million uplift in EBIT was largely fuel-driven; while Air France-KLM benefited from hedging, coverage stands at only ~50% for 2026 (Fig. 3), which could prove a headwind if prices turn.

Fig 3

There was no change in the yearly outlook (Fig. 4), and valuation looks broadly in line with the 2014–2019 average on a historical basis. Even so, we stay cautious given 1) a soft French macro, 2) unresolved KLM cabin-crew negotiations, and 3) uncertainty around RASK. With an enterprise value above €10 billion and a market cap below €3 billion, equity holders still face significant execution leverage.

Fig 4

Having said that, there were also positives in the quarter: 1) ex-fuel unit costs improved to +1.3% compared to a +3% in Q2 2025, 2) management now expects ex-fuel unit costs to end the year in the low single digits, 3) October RASK was positive, even if forward load factors trail last year, and 4) premium cabins continue to outpace economy. Additionally, with last year’s Olympic Games created a tough comparison; matching a strong Q3 was always a stretch.

Valuation

In our last analysis (ahead of the Q2 results), we lowered the company's core operating profit by 2% to €1.95 billion. With a YTD result of €1.36 billion and a supportive quarter for travel demand, we believe Air France-KLM will deliver. At this point, 2025 looks de-risked, but we are more cautious about 2026 given the trajectory of fuel costs, capacity expansion with a lower load factor, and still higher-for-longer interest rates. We would welcome improved disclosure and visibility from the top management team. Therefore, given our cautious approach, we now forecast a 2026 EBIT of €1.9 billion based on €34 billion in top-line sales. We made some adjustments to the forecasts based on yield expectations and capacity growth. This is slightly below the average consensus estimates of €2.19 billion. Considering a €400 million deleveraging backed by a solid FCF generation, we also lower our absolute value in interest rate expenses to €380 million. With a 20% corporate tax rate, our net income projection reached €1.22 billion. At the current outstanding shares, we derive an EPS of €4.6. The company trades at a P/E of 2.4x, which is appealing. However, if we look at France-KLM's valuation on EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT, the company trades at >3.5x and >6x, respectively. As already reported, post-COVID-19, commercial airlines have traded at an EV/EBITDA of 1-3x. Valuation also stretched during the previous COVID-19 period. Therefore, rolling forward our valuation, blending the EV/EBITDA of 3x with an EBITDA of €4.7 billion (considering D&A projection of €2.8 billion) and a P/E of 3x, we slightly lower our valuation from €11.5 to €10.65 per share ($1.24 for the AFLYY ADR). We maintain a neutral rating.

Risks

Air France-KLM risks include higher airport, security, and wage costs amid ongoing pilot wage negotiations. The company is exposed to lower demand and to higher taxes on ancillary services. Air France is progressing with the KLM turnaround, which might impact the company's P&L. Higher comps from low-cost players, FX evolution, and significant event risk, such as terrorist attacks, could significantly affect the company's margin evolution.

Conclusion

All in all, it was a positive quarter for Air France-KLM; however, the good results were explained by lower fuel costs, and we have limited visibility on 2026. At this stage, we prefer to remain neutral, with a preference for the Lufthansa Group.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta's Metaverse Rollback: Why Investors Are Buying The Pivot
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) surprised the Street to the good side on the morning of December 3. The $1.6 trillion market cap Communication Services company announced it planned to shed up to 30% of its metaverse workforce. Some casual investors may have been bewildered that the firm still had significant operations in CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s vision from years ago.

Recall that Meta changed its name from Facebook in October 2021, back when AI was hardly a major investment theme, and augmented reality was seen as the immediate tech innovation to pour capex dollars into. Much has changed in the past 50 months, and Meta has proven itself to be aggressive in the AI arms race. But that front-foot posture also comes with a degree of flexibility.

I see the share-price gain related to its metaverse step back as a positive. Focusing on AI, with an eye toward balance sheet stability, should help the Mag-7 name retain its foothold in the Comm Services sector. What’s more, it’s arguably the most attractive stock on a valuation basis (now that Alphabet (GOOGL) trades close to 30x earnings.

I have a Buy rating on META. I see shares as a solid value, and the retreat off its peak before the Q3 earnings report as an opportunity into 2026. The chart, however, shows some potential risk, and a bearish death cross could happen soon. I’ll explain why that may not be as scary as it sounds, in this case.

META Lagging The S&P 500 YTD

S&P 500 Forward P/E Ratio Heat Map: META Just 22.3x

Back in October, Meta reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q3 GAAP EPS of $1.05 missed the Wall Street consensus target by a wide margin, while revenue of $51.2 billion, up more than 26% from the same period a year ago, was a material $1.8 billion beat. The company confirmed that it expected to earn Q4 revenue of between $56-$59 billion, which was close to the consensus estimate. The Q3 miss was due to a large $16 billion one-time charge related to impacts from the new tax bill.

Shares plunged 11% in the session that followed, its worst reaction to an earnings event in more than three years, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services. Looking ahead to the Q4 report, the options market prices in a more modest 6.8% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the January 28 event.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Meta’s operational performance was solid, the tax-related write-down notwithstanding. It continues to use a significant amount of operating cash flow on AI capex. Still, the firm is free cash flow positive, which is an increasingly important metric to monitor among the hyperscalers. Ex currency swings, ad growth hums along at a firm 25% annual pace. I’d also call out that its adjusted EPS would have beaten estimates at $7.25 versus $6.74 expected.

Ad strength appears on track for the fourth quarter, too. Analysts, however, have grown increasingly concerned about Zuckerberg’s intensity when it comes to AI investments and overall expense growth. Gone are the days of the “year of efficiency,” and the market may be yearning for those days, based on the share-price move post-earnings.

Still, EPS growth estimates are high for the out year, while the P/E multiple is certainly not all that elevated. Elsewhere, Meta reported 3.5 billion daily active users across its apps, with Threads reaching 150 million daily actives.

I assert that a higher P/E is justified, based on Meta’s automated AI tools that now generate $60 billion in annual recurring revenue. Still, we’ll have to monitor higher depreciation expenses and AI-related hiring costs. Indeed, the past few months have seen a slew of extremely high salaries given to AI talent. I fully expect capex to increase in the quarters on tap, with emphasis on cloud storage and data centers.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to have slowed in fiscal 2025, but the out-year non-GAAP per-share earnings forecast is strong in the high teens. Beyond that, we are probably looking at a steady low-teens bottom-line growth pace.

Today, free cash flow per share is $17.78 over the past 12 months, which is almost a 3% FCF yield. Hence, profitability trends are impressive. Finally, the sellside was quick to downgrade their price targets shortly after the Q3 numbers hit the tape. Thus, analyst sentiment is by no means frothy.

Meta: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume a long-term EPS growth rate of 12% and grant Meta a 2x PEG ratio (near the S&P 500’s average), then the P/E multiple should be near 24x on a normalized basis. Apply that to forward EPS of $30, and the resulting fair value estimate is $720, making Meta shares undervalued.

META: A Reasonable P/E Multiple

Key risks for Meta focus on the aggressive spending intentions. If projects are not as profitable as forecasted, it could call into question the long-term viability of incremental AI infrastructure investments. What’s more, rising legal expenses, employee compensation, and potentially power/electricity costs could pressure net margins.

A bit under the radar in the Q3 report was the lowering of guidance for Reality Labs (although that’s now under the spotlight with the Metaverse job cuts)... that may weigh on year-on-year numbers looking ahead. Overseas, pressure builds from European Union regulators, which may dent international growth endeavors. High capex is likely to keep share buybacks in check, too.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, January 28 AMC. Before that, shares trade ex a $0.525 dividend on Monday, December 15.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares undervalued in my view, META’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the stock plunged toward a key gap level from the middle of the second quarter. I like the fact that shares did not penetrate the gap—that's a sign of the bulls holding their own. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph may be breaking out right now, and it comes as META encroaches on major resistance near $690.

But take a look at the short-term 50-day moving average. Shares were rejected at that trend-indicator line right at the open this morning. Additionally, a bearish death cross is imminent—a pattern in which the 50dma moves below the long-term 200dma. Why am I not too worried about that? Well, we saw a similar formation in May, just as META was in full-on recovery mode.

Another bullish point: The stock tagged with near precision its downside measured move price objective based on the head and shoulders topping pattern from the summer and early fall. So, the correction may already be over. Still, I’d like to see META rally through $690 from a momentum point of view. Support is at the November low.

META: Death Cross Imminent, Downside Target Already Hit

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on META. I see the Magnificent Seven stock as attractive on valuation, while the technical situation points to the potential for a continued rally off the November low.
